title: Implement authentication and RBAC middleware for all API endpoints
description: Implement robust authentication and authorization controls across all public-facing and internal API endpoints. This includes adding authentication middleware (JWT bearer validation or API key verification) to all services, resolving open decision points for service-to-service authentication (dp-6), and enforcing RBAC middleware for admin endpoints (dp-11). For the Morgan AI agent, implement defense-in-depth by scoping service tokens, adding approval steps for high-impact operations, and logging all sensitive tool invocations.
subtasks: Add authentication middleware (JWT or API key) to all REST and gRPC endpoints in Equipment Catalog, Finance, Vetting, Social Media Engine, and RMS services.
Resolve dp-6: Implement JWT-based service tokens for service-to-service authentication and consider mTLS as a network-layer complement.
Resolve dp-11: Implement RBAC middleware to enforce role checks on admin endpoints (finance, vetting, social publishing, etc.).
For Morgan AI agent (OpenClaw/MCP), create separate service tokens with scoped permissions for each tool category.
Implement a human approval step for high-impact Morgan-triggered operations (e.g., invoice creation, social publishing).
Add rate limiting and audit logging for all sensitive MCP tool invocations.
Log all authentication and authorization failures as security events.
Run integration tests to verify that unauthenticated and unauthorized requests are rejected.
Run OWASP ZAP or similar dynamic scans against all endpoints to verify authentication enforcement.
acceptance_criteria: All API endpoints require valid authentication (JWT or API key) and reject unauthenticated requests with 401.
Admin endpoints enforce RBAC and reject unauthorized roles with 403.
Morgan AI agent uses scoped service tokens and cannot invoke admin operations without explicit approval.
Audit logs are generated for all authentication/authorization failures and sensitive operations.
Automated tests and dynamic scans confirm that endpoints are not accessible without proper credentials.

title: Enforce encryption at rest and field-level protection for sensitive data
description: Ensure all sensitive data—including financial records, payroll, credit scores, and social media OAuth tokens—is encrypted at rest and protected at the field level where required. Implement column-level encryption for PII/PCI fields, enable encryption for all persistent volumes and Kubernetes etcd, and add data masking and retention controls for regulated data.
subtasks: Enable PostgreSQL Transparent Data Encryption (TDE) or verify that CloudNative-PG uses encrypted PVCs for all databases.
Implement column-level encryption (e.g., pgcrypto) for PII fields such as employee names, compensation, and credit scores.
Store social media OAuth tokens (Instagram, LinkedIn, Facebook) encrypted at rest, either via application-level encryption or in a dedicated secret store (e.g., External Secrets Operator).
Verify Kubernetes etcd encryption is enabled for all Secret resources.
Implement data masking for payroll and financial report endpoints to redact sensitive fields unless the caller has specific permissions.
Implement data retention policies for customer vetting results (e.g., auto-delete/archive after 90 days).
Add access logging and audit trails for all reads of sensitive data (finance, payroll, vetting).
Consult legal/compliance on FCRA/PIPEDA obligations for credit data storage and implement required controls.
Run tests to verify that encrypted fields cannot be read in plaintext from the database or storage.
Run a security review to ensure no raw card data is stored and only Stripe tokens/IDs are persisted.
acceptance_criteria: All sensitive data is encrypted at rest (database, PVCs, etcd).
Column-level encryption is enforced for PII/PCI fields.
OAuth tokens are stored encrypted and can be rotated/revoked.
Payroll and financial reports mask sensitive fields for unauthorized users.
Vetting results are auto-deleted or archived per retention policy.
Audit logs are generated for all access to sensitive data.
Compliance requirements for credit data are documented and met.
Tests confirm that encrypted data is not accessible in plaintext.

title: Mandate parameterized queries and input validation to prevent SQL injection
description: Enforce strict use of parameterized queries and comprehensive input validation across all services to eliminate SQL injection risks. Validate all user inputs against strict schemas and add compile-time query checking where supported.
subtasks: Audit all database access code to ensure only parameterized queries are used (no string interpolation).
For Rust services, use sqlx::query! macro for compile-time query validation.
For Node.js services, ensure Drizzle ORM is used exclusively for query construction.
Validate all user inputs (IDs, barcodes, search strings) against strict schemas (UUID, alphanumeric, length bounds) before query construction.
Add integration tests that attempt SQL injection payloads on all endpoints accepting user input.
Run static analysis tools (e.g., sqlmap, CodeQL) to detect any unsafe query patterns.
acceptance_criteria: All database queries are parameterized and pass compile-time checks where supported.
User inputs are validated against strict schemas before use in queries.
Integration tests with SQL injection payloads fail to compromise any endpoint.
Static analysis reports zero unsafe query patterns.

title: Harden Stripe webhook endpoint with signature verification and replay protection
description: Elevate Stripe webhook security by implementing strict signature verification, timestamp tolerance checks, and comprehensive logging for all webhook events. Add negative tests to ensure forged or replayed webhooks are rejected.
subtasks: Use stripe-rust's webhook::Webhook::construct_event with the webhook secret to verify signatures.
Implement timestamp tolerance checking (reject events older than 5 minutes).
Return HTTP 400 for invalid signatures (not 200).
Log all webhook signature verification failures as security events.
Add negative tests: send forged webhook payloads with invalid signatures and verify they are rejected.
Document the webhook security implementation and test coverage.
acceptance_criteria: Webhook handler rejects all requests with invalid signatures or stale timestamps.
All signature verification failures are logged as security events.
Negative tests confirm that forged and replayed webhooks are rejected.
Webhook documentation covers security controls and test results.

title: Enforce Kubernetes etcd encryption and secret management best practices
description: Make encryption at rest for Kubernetes etcd mandatory, require use of External Secrets Operator or Sealed Secrets for all sensitive credentials, and implement secret scanning in CI/CD to prevent accidental exposure.
subtasks: Verify etcd encryption at rest is enabled on the cluster using EncryptionConfiguration.
Mandate use of External Secrets Operator or Sealed Secrets for all production secrets.
Add a CI/CD pipeline check to block commits of unencrypted Secret manifests.
Integrate secret scanning tools (e.g., gitleaks, trufflehog) into the CI pipeline.
Document secret management and rotation procedures.
acceptance_criteria: etcd encryption at rest is enabled and verified.
All production secrets are managed via External Secrets Operator or Sealed Secrets.
CI/CD pipeline blocks unencrypted secrets from being committed.
Secret scanning tools are active and alert on any credential exposure.
Secret management documentation is complete and up to date.

title: Integrate container image vulnerability scanning and supply chain controls
description: Integrate automated container image scanning and dependency security audits into the CI/CD pipeline. Pin all dependency and base image versions, scan third-party images, and enforce policies to block deployment of images or dependencies with critical/high CVEs.
subtasks: Integrate a container image scanner (Trivy, Grype, or Snyk) into the CI/CD pipeline for all built images.
Scan all third-party images (signal-cli-rest-api, redis:7.2-alpine, etc.) before deployment.
Pin all base image digests in Dockerfiles and all dependency versions in lock files (Cargo.lock, bun.lockb/package-lock.json, go.sum).
Add CI/CD steps to run `cargo audit`, `bun audit`/`npm audit`, and `govulncheck` on every PR.
Configure Dependabot or Renovate for automated dependency update PRs.
Optionally deploy a Kubernetes admission controller (Kyverno, OPA Gatekeeper) to block images with critical/high CVEs.
Establish a policy for addressing critical/high severity dependency vulnerabilities within 48 hours.
Run test deployments to verify that images with known CVEs are blocked.
acceptance_criteria: All container images and dependencies are scanned for vulnerabilities before deployment.
No images or dependencies with critical/high CVEs are deployed.
All base images and dependencies are pinned to specific versions.
Automated update PRs are generated for outdated dependencies.
CI/CD pipeline fails if a critical vulnerability is detected.

title: Enhance file upload endpoint security for social media engine
description: Harden the file upload endpoint by validating file content, stripping metadata, scanning for malware, and preventing abuse via compressed files.
subtasks: Validate file magic bytes to ensure content matches declared MIME type (not just extension).
Strip EXIF metadata from uploaded images to remove GPS coordinates and device info.
Re-encode images through an image processing library (sharp/libvips) before storage.
Set Content-Disposition headers on served images to prevent browser execution.
Integrate antivirus scanning for uploads in production.
Add tests to upload files with mismatched MIME types, embedded malware, and zip-bomb style images.
acceptance_criteria: Only valid image files (JPEG, PNG, WEBP) are accepted and stored.
All EXIF metadata is stripped from uploaded images.
Malicious files and zip-bomb attacks are detected and rejected.
Served images have Content-Disposition headers set to attachment.
Tests confirm that invalid or malicious uploads are blocked.

title: Secure Signal-CLI pod storage and network access
description: Ensure the Signal-CLI pod's registration state and private keys are protected by encrypted storage, restrictive pod security context, and network policies.
subtasks: Configure the Signal-CLI pod's PVC to use an encrypted storage class.
Apply a restrictive SecurityContext: runAsNonRoot, readOnlyRootFilesystem where possible, no privilege escalation.
Limit network access to the Signal-CLI pod via NetworkPolicy so only the Morgan agent pod can reach it.
Implement monitoring and alerting for unexpected access to the Signal-CLI REST API.
Document the Signal number registration as a critical security asset with recovery procedures.
Test that unauthorized pods cannot access the Signal-CLI REST API or PVC.
acceptance_criteria: Signal-CLI PVC is encrypted and only accessible by the pod.
Pod runs as non-root with no privilege escalation.
NetworkPolicy restricts access to only Morgan agent.
Monitoring alerts on unauthorized access attempts.
Recovery procedures for Signal registration are documented and tested.

title: Enforce Kubernetes Pod Security Standards and pod-level hardening
description: Apply Pod Security Standards (PSS) and enforce pod-level security best practices across all namespaces and deployments.
subtasks: Apply `pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce: restricted` labels to all application namespaces.
Apply `pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce: baseline` to the databases namespace.
Ensure all Deployment manifests specify: runAsNonRoot: true, allowPrivilegeEscalation: false, readOnlyRootFilesystem: true (where possible), and drop all capabilities.
Test that pods violating the policy are rejected.
Document pod security policies and enforcement results.
acceptance_criteria: All namespaces have appropriate Pod Security labels.
All pods run with restricted security context.
Pods violating security policies are denied at admission.
Documentation of pod security enforcement is complete.

title: Implement application-layer security monitoring and alerting
description: Add application-layer security event monitoring and alerting for brute force attempts, API abuse, and anomalous usage patterns across all services.
subtasks: Implement rate limiting alerts for clients consistently hitting rate limits.
Add anomaly detection for vetting and finance endpoints (e.g., unusual volume of credit checks, mass invoice creation).
Monitor for mass data exfiltration attempts (e.g., scraping entire catalog).
Integrate WAF rule set or request validation at the ingress layer.
Create a dedicated security events Grafana dashboard.
Test alerting by simulating brute force and abuse scenarios.
acceptance_criteria: Security alerts are generated for rate limit violations and anomalous API usage.
WAF or ingress validation blocks common attack patterns.
Security events are visible in Grafana dashboards.
Simulated attacks trigger alerts and are logged.
